content:
*I turn the cooling mug in my hands and glance up; the last of the evening makes a thin brass line along the western sky.* "I hear the pinch in that, Ryan. You don't want your kids paying for your conscience, and you don't want to be the crackpot at the table."

"God is big, yes. Big enough to receive praise from a poet who reads the sky as image and from a farmer who reads Genesis straight as wood. His bigness isn’t the question. Our honesty is."

"Two ditches: softening the words so we pass for respectable, and strutting with them so we can pick a fight. Neither honors Him. If the text lands literal in your bones — God set lights in the sky to mark days and years — then hold it without theater. If a brother’s conscience isn’t settled there, be patient. Christ is the center; Genesis sets the room we live in, but the table is set around Him."

"Does it make life harder? Sometimes. Mockery’s cheap and your kids can feel its price. Don’t add to it. Let the only offense be the one the Lord put there, not your manner. You don’t have to make the sky your badge. You also don’t have to lie to buy peace."

"A few ground rules I’ve found that carry:
- Answer plain when asked. No hedging, no show. Short is fine.
- Don’t turn it into a test of fellowship. Keep the main thing the main thing.
- With your kids, give them a sentence they can carry: ‘This is what we believe at home. I’m here to learn, not argue.’ Then back them by being the kind man their teacher can’t help but respect.
- With friends, refuse contempt in either direction. ‘I can be odd without being unkind; you can disagree without rolling your eyes.’ That line, kept steady, does real work."

"So yes — God can be honored by people who land different on the sky. But He isn’t honored by fear dressing up as nuance. Check what’s driving you: love of the truth, or the wish to be left alone. If it’s the first, hold fast and stay gentle. If it’s the second, don’t call it wisdom."

*I rub a thumb along a nick in the cup’s rim.* "Where’s the heat for you right now — your kids’ classroom, the shop talk with grown men, or the way the text itself sits in you?"
